title: Quantum-geometric origin of superfluid weight in quasicrystals with critical states
authors: Kazuma Saito, Ryo Okugawa, Yusuke Kato, Takami Tohyama
abstract: A distinctive feature of many quasiperiodic systems is the presence of critical states that are neither extended nor exponentially localized. We investigate the geometric effect on the superfluid weight in quasiperiodic systems with critical states at zero temperature. We employ both real-space and momentum-space approaches to superfluid weight in quasicrystals, which allows us to separate the conventional and quantum geometric contributions. We find that the superfluid weight is dominated by the geometric contribution in quasiperiodic systems with critical states. This finding reveals a fundamental interplay between superconductivity and critical states in quasicrystals.
summary: This theoretical paper investigates how the geometric structure of quasicrystals influences their superconducting properties by calculating the superfluid weight. By separating the total weight into conventional and geometric parts, the authors demonstrate that in systems with critical electronic states, the quantum geometry provides the dominant contribution to superconductivity. This highlights a deep interplay between lattice topology and superconducting pairing mechanisms.
main_problem: The study investigates the geometric origin of superfluid weight in quasiperiodic systems that host critical electronic states at zero temperature.
main_result:
The research finds that the superfluid weight is dominated by a quantum geometric contribution ($	ext{D}_{\mu
u,	ext{geom}}$) in these quasicrystals, revealing a fundamental link between superconductivity and critical states.
method: The authors employ both real-space (Open Boundary Conditions) and momentum-space (Periodic Boundary Conditions) approaches to calculate the superfluid weight using the Kubo formula applied to the Bogoliubov-de Gennes Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The system under study is quasiperiodic, exemplified by the Ammann-Beenker quasicrystal or modeled via the Aubry-André-Harper (AAH) model. Superconductivity is analyzed using an s-wave pairing assumption within a Hubbard-type framework.
key_observables:
Superfluid weight ($	ext{D}_{\mu
u}$), conventional contribution ($	ext{D}_{\mu
u,	ext{conv}}$), and geometric contribution ($	ext{D}_{\mu
u,	ext{geom}}$).
important_parameters: Interaction strength ($U$), chemical potential ($	ilde{\mu}$), and the AAH model parameter $\lambda/t'$ (especially near the transition point $\lambda = 2t'$).
main_assumptions: The analysis separates the total superfluid weight into conventional and geometric parts, assuming s-wave pairing symmetry.
figures_summary: Figure 2(a) likely shows the energy spectrum and multifractal dimension ($D_2$) dependence on the AAH parameter $\lambda/t'$, illustrating the transition between different state regimes.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the superfluid weight calculation using both real-space (OBC) and momentum-space (PBC) techniques. It compares the conventional and geometric contributions, demonstrating that the geometric term dominates when critical states are present in the quasicrystal structure.
why_interesting: This work connects concepts from topological physics (geometric contributions to transport coefficients) with strongly correlated electron models (Hubbard model on quasiperiodic lattices), which is highly relevant for understanding exotic superconductivity mechanisms beyond simple mean-field theories.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The Ammann-Beenker quasicrystal with 2827 sites. Every vertex is the atomic site and each edge represents nearest-neighbor hopping link. We set the link length a to unity for these calculations. (b) The DOS per site of the tight-binding Hamiltonian without the interaction. (c) and (d) Superfluid weight obtained with (c) open boundary and (d) periodic boundary conditions at zero temperature as a function of |U|/t for each ˜µ.

FIG. 2. (a) Evolution of the energy spectrum of the AAH model. The color bar represents the multifractal dimension D2. (b) Conventional superfluid weight Dxx,conv (blue) and geometric one Dxx,geom (red) for U = −1.5t′ at zero temper- ature.
